---
title: "Visual Explanations of XAI Explainers"
author: "Janith Wanniarachchi"
subtitle: "Supervised by Dianne Cook, Patricia Menendez, Thiyanga Talagala and Kate Saunders"
format:
  revealjs:
    footer: "user-2026.janithwanni.com"
    theme: [default, user_theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    smaller: true
    history: false 
    embed-resources: false
    mermaid:
      theme: neutral
---

```{r}
source("src/preamble.R")
source("src/data.R")
source("src/model.R")
source("src/explainers.R")
source("src/tables.R")
source("src/plots.R")
```

## Hi I'm Janith

:::{.center}

* I'm a PhD candidate at Monash University, Australia 
* My research work is around making it easier to explain how machine learning models work. 

:::

## AI and Machine Learning is everywhere

:::{.incremental}
* Like it or not, there's a surge of models created to solve problems across domains.
* It feels easy to create these models with easy to access compute power and less restrictions on mapping the inputs and outputs
* But when these models are facing the public, and we can't understand how they work, that's going to be a problem!
* And that is where Explainable AI (XAI) methods come in.
:::

## Let's take an example,

You are working at the OddPoverty Bank where you help wombats get loans. 

and you were hired as a data science intern in the loan department,

You are given the following dataset to model, which will classify whether a wombat applying for a burrow hole loan is eligible or not.

it's a simple one for an intern, the dataset has two variables, how old the wombat is and how heavy the wombat is.

the training dataset looks like as follows,

```{r}
train_data_fig
```

## Time to get modeling,

You get around to making a model that works well, to double check you follow a model in data space visualisation like as follows

```{r}
model_preds_fig
```

## We have complaints! 

Three wombats came up upset that their loans were rejected, and they asked what were was the important thing that we considered to deny their loan for a burrowing hole.

```{r}
poi_data_fig
```

How do we explain this model to the wombats?

## We need ways to eXplain AI/ML methods   

Explainable AI methods or XAI methods came in as a solution to this problem. 

XAI can help you look at 

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width=50%}

<p style = "text-align:center"> <img src="imgs/global_methods_2.png" style="width:90%"/> </p>

How the model reacts to different features overall using <span class="accent-color"> Global Interpretability Methods </span>

:::

::: {.column width=50%}

<p style = "text-align:center"> <img src="imgs/local_methods_2.png" style="width:90%"/> </p>

How the model gives a prediction to one single instance using <span class="accent-color">Local Interpretability Methods </span>

::: 

::::

## Explaining one prediction

There are several key <span class = "accent-color"> local interpretability methods </span> that are related to each other in how they approach the problem

1. LIME (Local Interpretable Model agnostic Explanations)
2. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)
2. Anchors
3. Counterfactuals

## Using XAI methods 

So let's pump these in to these methods and let's help our wombats out!

```{r}
disagree_table
```

Wait so which one do we tell these three wombats?

## Algorithmic Disagreements

Why did this workflow fail

* Different XAI methods probe different mathematical facets of a model.
* When you run multiple methods on the same pipeline, they **frequently yield conflicting explanations**.

We must manually reconcile abstract mathematical formulas with raw numerical outputs.

## The Isolation Problem

Despite XAI advances, current state-of-the-art tools operate in a vacuum.

* We don't see how these explanations relate to the data itself or the model's actual internal view of the data. 
* There's more to the story than just the final explanations 

We are left navigating a fragmented, disconnected ecosystem.

## Let's break it apart

XAI explanations are just the tip of the iceberg

There are more things that came together to make these XAI explanations. 

```{=html}
<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/jointjs/3.7.7/joint.min.css" />
<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/jquery/3.6.4/jquery.min.js"></script>
<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/lodash.js/4.17.21/lodash.min.js"></script>
<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/backbone.js/1.4.1/backbone-min.js"></script>
<script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/jointjs/3.7.7/joint.min.js"></script>

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; width: 100%; margin-top: 20px;">
    <div id="xai-workflow-canvas" style="background: #ffffff; border-radius: 8px; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);"></div>
</div>

<script>
$(document).ready(function() {
    var namespace = joint.shapes;
    var graph = new joint.dia.Graph({}, { cellNamespace: namespace });

    var paper = new joint.dia.Paper({
        el: document.getElementById('xai-workflow-canvas'),
        model: graph,
        width: 800,
        height: 650,
        gridSize: 10,
        drawGrid: false,
        background: { color: 'transparent' },
        cellNamespace: namespace,
        interactive: true // Set to true if you want to drag elements during the presentation
    });

    // Helper: Create Subgraph Backgrounds
    function createSubgraph(id, label, x, y, width, height) {
        return new joint.shapes.standard.Rectangle({
            id: id,
            position: { x: x, y: y },
            size: { width: width, height: height },
            attrs: {
                body: { fill: '#f8f9fa', stroke: '#adb5bd', strokeWidth: 2, strokeDasharray: '5,5', rx: 10, ry: 10 },
                label: { text: label, fill: '#6c757d', fontSize: 14, fontWeight: 'bold', refY: 15, textAnchor: 'middle' }
            }
        });
    }

    // Helper: Create Nodes
    function createNode(id, label, x, y) {
        return new joint.shapes.standard.Rectangle({
            id: id,
            position: { x: x, y: y },
            size: { width: 240, height: 60 },
            attrs: {
                body: { fill: '#ffffff', stroke: '#0d6efd', strokeWidth: 2, rx: 5, ry: 5 },
                label: { text: label, fill: '#212529', fontSize: 14, textWrap: { width: 220, height: 50 } }
            }
        });
    }

    // Helper: Create Links
    function createLink(sourceId, targetId) {
        return new joint.shapes.standard.Link({
            source: { id: sourceId },
            target: { id: targetId },
            router: { name: 'manhattan' }, // Creates orthogonal lines
            connector: { name: 'rounded' },
            attrs: {
                line: { stroke: '#495057', strokeWidth: 2, targetMarker: { 'type': 'path', 'd': 'M 10 -5 0 0 10 5 Z' } }
            }
        });
    }

    // 1. Define Subgraphs (Containers)
    var subInput = createSubgraph('subInput', 'Input Space', 40, 20, 720, 120);
    var subStep1 = createSubgraph('subStep1', '1. Perturbation Mechanism', 40, 170, 720, 120);
    var subStep2 = createSubgraph('subStep2', '2. Optimization Objective', 240, 320, 320, 120);
    var subStep3 = createSubgraph('subStep3', '3. Final Output', 240, 470, 320, 120);

    // 2. Define Nodes
    var nodeA = createNode('A', 'Trained Black-Box Model', 80, 60);
    var nodeB = createNode('B', 'Local Observation (x)', 480, 60);
    var nodeD = createNode('D', 'Compute Black-Box Mapping', 80, 210);
    var nodeC = createNode('C', 'Generate Local Neighbors', 480, 210);
    var nodeE = createNode('E', 'Apply Optimization Criteria', 280, 360);
    var nodeF = createNode('F', 'XAI Explanation', 280, 510);

    // 3. Embed nodes into subgraphs (Ensures they move together if interactiveness is enabled)
    subInput.embed(nodeA); subInput.embed(nodeB);
    subStep1.embed(nodeC); subStep1.embed(nodeD);
    subStep2.embed(nodeE);
    subStep3.embed(nodeF);

    // Add elements to graph (Subgraphs first so they render behind nodes)
    graph.addCells([subInput, subStep1, subStep2, subStep3, nodeA, nodeB, nodeC, nodeD, nodeE, nodeF]);

    // 4. Define and add Links
    var links = [
        createLink('B', 'C'),
        createLink('A', 'D'),
        createLink('C', 'D'),
        createLink('C', 'E'),
        createLink('D', 'E'),
        createLink('E', 'F')
    ];
    graph.addCells(links);
});
</script>
```

## The Baseline Predictive Workflow

Before explaining, we must map standard predictive model design.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart LR
    subgraph Prediction Workflow
    direction LR
      A["Dataset"] --> B["Training Data"]
      A --> C["Testing Data"]
      
      B --> D["Train Model"]
      D --> E["Trained Model"]
      
      C --> F["Scoring & Evaluation"]
      E --> F
    end

```

## Setting the Stage for Local Explanations

When shifting from *prediction* to *explanation*, the core question changes:

> "Which features were most influential in giving the current prediction for this specific observation?"

* Highly non-linear boundaries require **local** analysis.
* **Model-agnostic** choices ensure the framework survives even as old models become obsolete.

## The 3-Stage Explanatory Abstraction

All local, model-agnostic XAI tools can be simplified into three common blocks:

::: {.incremental}

1. **Perturbation Mechanism**
* Generates synthetic observations in the neighborhood of the target observation (e.g., masking, local sampling).


2. **Optimization Objective**
* Evaluates black-box mappings against a criterion (surrogate loss, axioms, distance metrics).


3. **Final Output**
* Returns an interpretable form (coefficients, rules, counterfactuals).
:::

# Visualising XAI methods

## SHAP

## LIME

## Counterfactuals

## Anchors

## In conclusion,

> My key take-away message is: Visualsing XAI methods requires us to look at all parts of the pipeline.
>
> We hope you that you get to use our proposed ideas and packages in the future when you are trying to explain models.
